response: Architects program to interfaces because they often face a lack of certainty about future object relationships, allowing new implementations to emerge without altering existing code. Unlike abstract classes, which enforce a rigid inheritance path, interfaces enable loose coupling where unrelated classes can share behavior dynamically. This flexibility is crucial when the full scope of the system is unknown, much like how a file might be accessed through different paths depending on the environment. Even if a salad of diverse components seems chaotic initially, defining contracts early ensures they can be mixed and matched later without breaking the whole.
